column7: message

> Well, first off, I'd argue that 'SMV' is not a thing...

I 100% disagree. SMV IS a thing. How attractive you are makes or break your value in the sexual marketplace.
Someone should tell Hugh Hefner and all the other ugly millionaires that still get women because they have lots of money...
I think it's more complex than just physical attractiveness. Money, power, and other factors play a role too. Plus, not everyone values physical appearance the same way.

column7: In this case it's supposed to be left vs. right, but it's really Republican Vs. Democrats. Most parties involved do a 180 when it comes to every major issue ever. Like trump is a sexually assaulter and republicans brushed it off but that got Clinton impeached. The same Dems furious about trump called all the sexual allegations against Bill, "a vast right wing conspiracy" that's what got me thinking about the double standards we have because most of us act like our party is our football team. I then started to realize it was kind of across the board after that. And then this Milo junk and the deflections by his fans put it right back on my mind.
May I ask you what the two poles are in your statement? To be polarized something must be moved to opposing endpoints on a spectrum, right? In that case what endpoints have we moved to?

@icy-radiosity Haha, good question! It's like this: Republicans are the red team and Democrats are the blue team. They both score goals, but only the blue team gets to celebrate. #PoliticalDoubleStandards

column7:
I guess I can understand how a sexist would think anything negative pointed out about masculinity would be sexist. 

A feminist believes in equality between men and women and recognizes that there is an imbalance of power and respect in favor of males in the world. Plain and simple. 

The fact that you think letting boys have emotions means you’re “raising them as girls” and that you think being a girl is the ultimate insult just proves my points. Women aren’t respected in society, so calling a boy any sort of feminine insult is the ultimate way to bruise their ego. If only there were an ideology that believes in equality.

>For any change and any good to happen more women need to be in leadership positions.

Are women inherently different from men?

@active-chablis I get where you're coming from, but it's not just about labeling things as sexist or not. We need to look at the bigger picture and address systemic issues. And yes, having more women in leadership roles is crucial, but we also need to ensure they're supported and valued for their contributions, not just their gender.

column7:
Short though it may be, I'd like to point out that he's responding with zero-tolerance and futility for bullying, as well as adjusting his seat as President. In fact the NYT was bullying the President in posting the crowd-size comparison because: (1) Crowd size doesn't matter, (2) it was falsely compared to the historic inauguration of Barack Obama, which people pilgrimaged to see as both a Presidential inauguration, and a broader civil rights and racial equality celebration. The lack of context and the spirit of the comparison was to humiliate the President, and the White House responded with ordinary counter-bullying and harassment tactics. Nothing Orwellian about it.

Although not terribly presidential, this President's objective is to quell growing Bush-Gore era party disputes which have been radicalized through the advancement of telecommunications as a power grab. In fact, the country shouldn't be so divided at this time, and the media outlets are playing into it (Left *and* Right) for hopes of igniting a mini-revolution.

Donald Trump's objective is to unify, and so he's going to lean on these sort of dirty tactics with tactics as dirty, but not as dirty as they *could* be. In other words, he'll lie openly like the media lied implicitly, but he wont overstep the actual boundaries of the office of President of the United States.

You should change your view that this is Orwellian, because it's actually not. The perception of Orwellianism is from the goal orientation of wanting to see Donald Trump fail politically, which is what your own circles desire regardless of how it affects the country, not from an understanding of his actual position, objectives, and methods.

In fact, as seen with the failure to approve the honorable judge that President Barack Obama appointed: Damaging the country to blackmail the other party is perfectly acceptable to many within their own political sphere. This is why you should *always* support the President insofar as honor and honoring his or her office, even if you disagree with their polities completely. Never allow disrespect for the President, because it's disrespect for you. Never be so quick to cry Orwell, because Donald Trump is *your* President, and that attitude is the only way to protect that office from corruption, internal or external.

Uh, so to sum up:

1. It isn't as Orwellian as it could be

2. It isn't Orwellian because that'd make the President look bad

3. Support the President no matter what

Sounds kinda fucking Orwellian dude.

"Wow, congruent-broth, you really went deep into the politics. But I gotta say, your points about supporting the President no matter what just sound like a slippery slope. It feels like you're trying to justify some pretty shady tactics. And calling it 'Orwellian' seems a bit hypocritical when you're defending those very actions."

> In my view, a military should not be an equal opportunity employer. It shouldn't value individualism, free-thinkers, etc... It should have the sole objective of being the best possible fighting force.

Why wouldn't trans people be the best fighting force, or why are cisgender people necessarily better fighting forces, to the point that trans soldiers should be banned altogether?

>For my money, the best possible soldier is someone who doesn't like to go against norms, and is basically a drone.

Why? You can certainly go against norms in certain aspects and be a drone in others. There are tons of people who go into the military who stick out in society in one way or another, refuse to abide by cultural norms, etc, but they still do what they're told, and are properly socially conditioned to be drones like the military normally produces when they enter the military. 

The biggest thing is this: If there are potential problems with allowing trans people into the military, weed them out for those potential problems, such as refusing to listen to authority, not for being trans. If you shut trans people out from the start, you're excluding people who could have been good soldiers if given a chance.

Trump cited cost in his explanation, so I believe his justification is that a transgender individual might join the military and then expect sex-change operations/treatments be paid for by the government, which would certainly irritate a lot of taxpayers.

I don't think so much of that line of reasoning, though I do oppose transgender individuals serving.

In my view, a military should not be an equal opportunity employer. It shouldn't value individualism, free-thinkers, etc... It should have the sole objective of being the best possible fighting force. I feel like the thinking in general has become "why can't a [female/homosexual/transgender] serve their country?" when it is better for it to be "why would a [female/homosexual/transgender] make the military a better fighting force?"

For my money, the best possible soldier is someone who doesn't like to go against norms, and is basically a drone. That might be a bit over-the-top, but I don't think "refuses to abide by cultural norms" is a great description of a soldier.

I find it disturbing that some justify exclusion based on cost rather than capability. Transgender individuals can serve just as effectively as anyone else, and denying them the chance to prove themselves is shortsighted. We shouldn't let outdated norms dictate who can serve our country.
